label: Sparse Retrieval
definition: Retrieval that represents queries and documents as high-dimensional sparse vectors over the vocabulary and ranks by term overlap, the classic keyword approach typified by BM25, strong at exact and rare-term matching.

body.text: Sparse retrieval ranks documents by the words they share with the query. Each document is treated as a very high-dimensional vector with one dimension per vocabulary term, almost all of them zero, with nonzero weights only for the words that actually appear; "sparse" names that mostly-empty representation. Matching is a matter of overlapping nonzero dimensions, weighted so that rarer, more informative words count more. This is the lineage of classic search engines, and its most enduring algorithm is BM25, a term-frequency scoring function that remains a strong, hard-to-beat baseline decades on.

The weighting is where the craft lives. A naive count of shared words rewards long documents and common terms unfairly, so sparse methods scale term frequency with diminishing returns, downweight terms that appear in many documents (the idea behind inverse document frequency), and normalize for document length. BM25 packages these adjustments into one well-tuned formula. Because the vectors are sparse, the index can be an inverted index, a map from each term to the documents containing it, which makes lookups extremely fast and is the structure web search has used for decades. Newer "learned sparse" models keep the sparse, term-indexed form but let a neural model set and even expand the term weights.

Sparse retrieval matters because exact lexical matching is genuinely the right tool for a large class of queries, the ones dense retrieval handles worst. A specific product code, a rare proper noun, an error string, a precise function name: here the user wants the literal token, and a keyword match is both more reliable and fully interpretable, since you can see exactly which terms caused a hit. Its weakness is the mirror of that strength. With no notion of meaning, it misses synonyms and paraphrases, so a query for "car" will not match a document that only says "automobile". That vocabulary-mismatch gap is precisely what dense retrieval was built to close.

Because the two methods fail in opposite ways, they are natural partners, and combining them is hybrid search: run sparse and dense retrieval together and fuse their rankings to get both exact-term precision and semantic recall. This site's retrieval is dense-first, built on voyage-3 embeddings in Pinecone, but understanding sparse retrieval is what explains the trade-off: it is why a purely semantic surface can stumble on an exact identifier, and why production search systems so often keep a lexical scorer like BM25 in the mix.
